54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
177	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
178	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
179	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
180	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
181	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
182	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
184	
185	## Reading references
186	
187	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
188	
189	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
190	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
191	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
192	
193	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
194	
195	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
196	
197	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
198	
199	Conditions for dispatch:
200	
201	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
202	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
203	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
204	
205	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
206	
207	```
208	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
209	  --name reference-retriever \
210	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
211	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
212	  --write-domain 'references/**'
213	```
214	
215	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
216	
217	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).

235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then

240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[

435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
441	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
443	  
444	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
445	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
446	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
447	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
448	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
449	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
450	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
451	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
452	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
453	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
455	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
456	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
457	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
458	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
459	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
460	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
461	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
462	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
463	  \[
464	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
465	  \]
466	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
467	\end{lemma}
468	
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
472	  exact sequence
473	  \[
474	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
475	  \]
476	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
477	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
478	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
479	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
480	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
481	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
482	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
483	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
484	  \[
485	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
486	  \]
487	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
488	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
489	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
490	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
491	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
492	  \begin{multline*}
493	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
494	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
495	  \end{multline*}
496	  Two ingredients close the proof:
497	
498	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
499	  hypothesis: by REF,
500	  \[
501	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
502	  \]
503	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
504	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
505	
506	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
507	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
508	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
509	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
510	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
511	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
512	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
513	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
514	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
515	
516	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
517	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
518	  \[
519	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
520	  \]
521	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
522	
523	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
524	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
525	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
526	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
527	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
528	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
529	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
530	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
531	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
532	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
533	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
534	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
535	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
536	  complex degenerates).
537	\end{proof}

849	  \(\texttt{principal}\,f\,h_f = 0\). The principal divisor is
850	  \(\sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot [Q]\), summed over prime divisors
851	  \(Q\in C.\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}\); the vanishing of the principal
852	  divisor is therefore equivalent to \(\ord_Q(f)=0\) for every prime
853	  divisor \(Q\) of \(C\). Hence \(f\) has no zeros and no poles on
854	  \(C\), and the inclusion
855	  \(\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\) places \(f\) inside
856	  the subsheaf of regular functions on \(C\), i.e.\
857	  \(f\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\). On a complete geometrically
858	  irreducible integral scheme over the algebraically closed field
859	  \(\bar k\) (in particular: smooth proper integral curve), the
860	  ring of global sections of the structure sheaf is exactly the base
861	  field,
862	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks

863	  Project tag \texttt{01XU}); the non-vanishing \(f\ne 0\) plus the
864	  fact that all elements of \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}(\top)\) coincide
865	  with \(\bar k^{\times}\) (the units refinement, Stacks Project tag
866	  \texttt{02P0}) places \(f\in \bar k^{\times}\subset K(C)\).
867	
868	  We now derive a contradiction from the hypothesis
869	  \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Set \(c:=f\in\bar k\), so
870	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot 1 = 0\) in \(K(C)\), i.e.\
871	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot \iota(s_1) = 0\) (since \(\iota(s_1)=1\), the
872	  defining equation \texttt{hs1 : toFunctionField s\(_1\) = 1} at
873	  line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}). By the \(\bar k\)-linearity of

\begin{lemma}
[Sub-claim (c): principal divisor of a non-constant rational function
on a complete curve is non-zero]
  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective,
        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P\in C\) be a
  closed point with coheight witness, let
  \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) be a global section satisfying
  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (with \(s_1\) the distinguished constant
  section, iter-195 setup line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}), and set
  \(f:=\iota(s)\) with \(h_f\colon f\ne 0\) supplied by
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}. Then
  the principal Weil divisor of \(f\) is non-zero:
  \[
    \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,(X:=C.\mathrm{left})\,f\,h_f
    \;\ne\;
    0 \quad \text{in } \mathrm{Div}(C.\mathrm{left}).
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
  Argue by the contrapositive. Suppose for contradiction that
  \(\texttt{principal}\,f\,h_f = 0\). The principal divisor is
  \(\sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot [Q]\), summed over prime divisors
  \(Q\in C.\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}\); the vanishing of the principal
  divisor is therefore equivalent to \(\ord_Q(f)=0\) for every prime
  divisor \(Q\) of \(C\). Hence \(f\) has no zeros and no poles on
  \(C\), and the inclusion
  \(\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\) places \(f\) inside
  the subsheaf of regular functions on \(C\), i.e.\
  \(f\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\). On a complete geometrically
  irreducible integral scheme over the algebraically closed field
  \(\bar k\) (in particular: smooth proper integral curve), the
  ring of global sections of the structure sheaf is exactly the base
  field,
  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks
  Project tag \texttt{01XU}); the non-vanishing \(f\ne 0\) plus the
  fact that all elements of \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}(\top)\) coincide
  with \(\bar k^{\times}\) (the units refinement, Stacks Project tag
  \texttt{02P0}) places \(f\in \bar k^{\times}\subset K(C)\).

  We now derive a contradiction from the hypothesis
  \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Set \(c:=f\in\bar k\), so
  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot 1 = 0\) in \(K(C)\), i.e.\
  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot \iota(s_1) = 0\) (since \(\iota(s_1)=1\), the
  defining equation \texttt{hs1 : toFunctionField s\(_1\) = 1} at
  line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}). By the \(\bar k\)-linearity of
  \(\iota\) (witnesses \texttt{htF\_smul} at line~1369 and
  \texttt{htF\_add} at line~1378 of the iter-195 helper body),
  \(\iota(s - c\cdot s_1) = 0\). By
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}, the
  map \(\iota\) is injective, so \(s - c\cdot s_1 = 0\), i.e.\
  \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting the iter-195
  hypothesis \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Hence \(\texttt{principal}\,
  f\,h_f \ne 0\), as required.

  \emph{Mathlib substrate consumed.} The statement
  ``global sections of \(\mathcal O_C\) on a complete integral curve
  over \(\bar k\) are exactly \(\bar k\)'' is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 /
  Stacks~\texttt{01XU} input; its units refinement (Stacks~\texttt{02P0})
  is direct since the constants \(\bar k\) form a field. At the
  pinned Mathlib commit the relevant entry-point is the
  project's existing function-field-of-curve identification + the
  proper + integral + algebraically-closed-base typeclass package
  available in the helper's local context. If a self-contained
  project-side lemma \texttt{Scheme.functionField\_of\_complete\_curve\_const}
  (or equivalent) is not yet available, the iter-196 prover lands a
  small named helper for this fact (estimated \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) LOC
  for the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement) and consumes it here.

  \emph{Estimated formalisation footprint.} \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) Lean
  LOC for the contrapositive setup, the
  ``no-zeros-or-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' step, the constants
  identification, and the final linear-independence contradiction.
  The kbar-linearity witnesses (\texttt{htF\_smul},
  \texttt{htF\_add}, \texttt{htF\_zero}) are already in scope
  (lines~1364--1387 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}); the injectivity hypothesis
  is consumed from
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sub-claim (c) substrate: a rational function with zero order at every
prime divisor on a complete curve is constant
(Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0})]
  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Let \(f \in K(C)\) be
  a nonzero rational function such that
  \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in C\). Then \(f\)
  lies in the image of the constant inclusion
  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\): there exists \(c \in \bar k\) with
  \[
    f \;=\; \texttt{algebraMap}\,\bar k\,K(C)\,c.
  \]
\end{lemma}

% NOTE (iter-197 rename — substrate vs.\ contrapositive). The original
% iter-196 phrasing of sub-claim~(c) was the contrapositive form
% ``principal divisor of a non-constant rational function on a complete
% curve is non-zero'':
%   \mathtt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,f\,h_f \;\ne\; 0
% whenever \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) and \(f = \iota(s)\). The two
% forms are mathematically equivalent: \(\mathrm{principal}\,f =
% \sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot[Q]\), so \(\mathrm{principal}\,f = 0\) iff
% \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q\), and the substrate
% above then forces \(f \in \bar k\). The iter-196 prover chose to
% extract the Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0} form as the
% standalone named substrate — it cleanly isolates the substantive
% Mathlib gap (``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete
% geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)'') — and inlined the
% contrapositive ``\(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1 \Rightarrow
% \mathrm{principal}\,f \ne 0\)'' wrapper inside
% \texttt{exists\_nonconstant\_rational\_from\_dim\_eq\_two}
% (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean} lines~1596--1635).
% The contrapositive runs as follows: assume \(\mathrm{principal}\,f
% = 0\); apply the substrate to obtain \(f = c \in \bar k\); use
% \(\bar k\)-linearity of \(\iota\) plus \(\iota(s_1) = 1\) and
% injectivity of \(\iota\)
% (\cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}) to
% conclude \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting
% \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\).

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:

  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).

  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
  sheaf coincides with the base field,
  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.

  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries
  a single typed sorry pending upstream development; the named-helper
  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
  iterations.
\end{proof}

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
ocofp-pin-cleanup-iter197

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapter
`blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex`

## Summary

Repaired the two broken `\lean{...}` pins introduced in iter-196 by
re-anchoring each sub-claim block on the named Lean declaration that
the iter-196 prover actually landed. Sub-claim (b)'s lemma is now
pinned to the existing private helper
`globalSections_iff_mpr` (OCofP.lean L1024) under a renamed label
`lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff_mpr`, with its
statement rewritten to match the helper's signature (existence-of-
section hypothesis ⇒ order conditions) and a short carrier-membership
proof sketch. Sub-claim (c)'s lemma is now pinned to the existing
private helper `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero`
(OCofP.lean L1390) under a renamed label, with its statement
re-stated as the Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~02P0 substrate
(`∀Q, ord_Q f = 0 ⇒ ∃ c, f = algebraMap kbar K(C) c`); the original
"principal ≠ 0 of nonconstant" contrapositive prose is preserved
verbatim in a `% NOTE:` block immediately after the new lemma,
explaining the equivalence and pointing to the inlined wrapper in
`exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`. M-3 and M-4 required
no edits (both were already correct in the on-disk chapter).

## Items addressed

### M-1 — broken pin `order_conditions_of_globalSection`
- **Action**: Option (b), as planner-recommended. Rewrote the lemma
  block (chapter L771-L821) to pin the existing helper
  `globalSections_iff_mpr`. New label
  `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff_mpr`. Statement
  now matches the helper's signature (witness section ⇒ order
  bounds). Proof block rewritten to a short carrier-submodule
  membership-projection sketch (no `\leanok` added — that is
  `sync_leanok`'s job).
- **`\uses{...}` cleanup**: the chapter's only other reference to
  the old label `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection`
  lived in the M-2 lemma's `\uses{...}` (chapter L829), which was
  replaced wholesale by the M-2 rewrite.

### M-2 — broken pin `WeilDivisor.principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant`
- **Action**: Renamed pin to
  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero`,
  matching the iter-196 extracted private helper (OCofP.lean L1390).
  New label
  `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero`.
  Statement restated as the Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~02P0
  substrate. Proof block rewritten to a two-step Hartshorne~I.3.4 /
  Stacks~0BCK + algebraic-closure sketch (matching the documentation
  block above the Lean private helper).
- **`% NOTE:` block**: Added immediately after the new lemma block,
  documenting that the contrapositive ``principal divisor of a
  nonconstant rational function is nonzero'' is the iter-196 original
  framing, mathematically equivalent to the substrate above, and was
  inlined inside `exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`
  (OCofP.lean L1596-1635). The note also records the original
  contrapositive proof shape in a compact form for posterity.

### M-3 — confirm `toFunctionField_injective` pin
- **Action**: No edit needed. Chapter L672 already carries
  `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField_injective}`,
  which matches the private Lean declaration at OCofP.lean L1287
  (namespace path: `AlgebraicGeometry → Scheme → lineBundleAtClosedPoint`,
  confirmed by namespace-open scan).

- **Action**: No edit needed. Chapter L692-L695 has the canonical

          def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}

  `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff_mpr`; re-pinned to

No external `references/<file>.md` files opened this session: the
edits were pure pin-renames + a textual `% NOTE:` block, with no new

`% SOURCE:` / `% SOURCE QUOTE:` / `\textit{Source: ...}` blocks

None.

None.

  by ~30 lines net, well within the directive's ≤~30 LOC delta

- Both renamed pins target **private** Lean declarations

  `leanblueprint`'s `\lean{...}` resolution treats private and
